I travel for work 6-8 times a year and my company's policy says nothing about which neighbourhoods to avoid, how to handle hotel room security, or what to do if something happens. Has anyone pushed for a better corporate travel safety policy?

author: Divya
content:
Seoul solo — safe yes, but what about the hidden camera situation in public bathrooms? How real is it in 2026?

South Korea has had documented issues with hidden cameras in public bathrooms, changing rooms, and guesthouses — so documented that the government launched a major inspection programme. I'm going to Seoul for 10 days solo and I want to know the current situation in 2025-2026. Is this still a real and active concern or has the crackdown made a genuine difference? How do you check a bathroom or accommodation for hidden cameras?

author: Lauren
content:
Wellness retreat vs solo travel — is the retreat model actually better for solo women who want community?

The women-only retreat trend is exploding — Ladies Who Lit, Wild Terrains, women's reading retreats in Morocco, yoga retreats in Rishikesh, spiritual trips in Bali. 20% of solo female travellers took a women-only group trip in 2025. But retreats are expensive and structured in ways that feel constraining to me. If I want community without losing independence — is a retreat the answer or is there a middle way that gives you the social payoff without the full package commitment?

author: Pooja
content:
Flying solo into a new country at night — how do you make the airport arrival safe?

53% of solo female travellers book direct flights even if more expensive specifically to avoid late-night arrivals according to 2025 data. But sometimes a cheaper late-night flight is genuinely the only realistic option. What's your complete protocol for arriving solo at night in a new country? Pre-booked transfer, specific apps, what you have pre-downloaded, what you avoid doing in the arrival hall, how you get from airport to accommodation without getting into an unmarked taxi.

author: Naomi
content:
The 'dead zone travel' trend — is solo travel to uncrowded, remote places actually safer or more risky for women?

The 2026 SoFe Travel guide mentions the 'dead-zone travel' trend — places like the Azores, remote Sri Lanka, off-season Iceland. Less crowded sounds appealing. But less crowded also means fewer people around if something goes wrong, less reliable transport, and less developed safety infrastructure. As a solo woman, do you feel safer in genuinely remote places with no crowds or does the crowd itself feel protective?

author: Nisha
content:
The hushpitality trend — slow, quiet, restorative solo travel. Is this actually what we want or is it just burnout rebranded?

2026 travel trend reports are all talking about 'hushpitality' — fjord villages, silent retreats, slow coastal stays, destinations that offer genuine quiet. Norway, Slovenia, and the Azores keep coming up. I'm deeply attracted to this but I'm also wondering if what I actually need is rest at home, not rest in an expensive foreign country. Has anyone done this kind of intentionally quiet solo trip and did it actually deliver?

author: Linda
content:
What's the real cost of travelling safely as a solo woman — the stuff that never appears in budget breakdowns?

Nobody puts the safety tax in their budget posts. But it's real. The extra ₹800 for a private room over a dorm because the dorm felt wrong. The Uber instead of the bus because it was dark. The restaurant that costs twice as much because it's in a safer neighbourhood. The travel insurance premium. The anti-theft bag. The eSIM instead of hunting for a SIM in a new city at 11pm. What does staying safe as a solo woman actually add to your daily travel budget?

author: Sneha
content:
I've taken 10+ solo trips and I'm finally less scared — what actually changed and when did it shift for you?

Research shows that safety worries drop from 78% on first trips to 59% after 10+ solo trips. I've done 12 now and I can feel the difference — but I can't always articulate what changed. Is it just exposure? Is it specific skills? Is it knowing you've handled hard things before and survived? For women who've crossed that threshold — what actually shifted?

author: Anna
content:
Travelling solo as an introvert — does it actually suit us better or are we just as drained by the social performance of solo travel?

I keep reading that solo travel suits introverts because you control the pace. And partly that's true. But solo travel also requires constant social management — navigating touts, asking for help, meeting people at hostels, eating alone in restaurants where staff try to fill the silence. I find this exhausting in a way that being with a travel partner removes. Is there a version of solo travel that actually works for deep introverts?

author: Riya
title: Walked back from Pier 7 to JBR Walk solo at 11:45pm — here's what I noticed
content:
Spent the evening at Pier 7 with friends, decided to walk back to my hotel near JBR Walk instead of grabbing a Careem. About 25 minutes on foot.

What surprised me: Marina Walk was busier at 11:45pm than most Indian cities at 8pm. Couples, families with kids in strollers, women in everything from abayas to sundresses, joggers. Lighting is genuinely bright the entire stretch — no dark gaps. Police patrol cars pass every 7–10 minutes.

The one stretch that felt off: the underpass near the Address Marina hotel. Two minutes long but quiet, with a couple of men loitering. I sped up and didn't make eye contact. Out the other side everything was normal again. Next time I'd walk over the bridge instead.

Nobody approached me, nobody catcalled, nobody stared in the way you can feel. I held my phone showing Google Maps in one hand and felt completely fine. Would do it again — but if I'd had drinks I'd take Careem (AED 12 the whole stretch).

author: Pooja
title: I'm 41, just spent 5 days solo in Dubai — here's what worked when you're not into clubs
content:
Most Dubai content is aimed at 25-year-olds doing rooftop bars and beach clubs. I was bracing for a city that wouldn't have anything for me. It absolutely did.

What worked:
• Stayed in Downtown (Rove Downtown, AED 380/night) — walkable to the Dubai Mall, fountain show, and Old Town. Felt central without being a party zone.
• Did the Sheikh Mohammed Centre cultural breakfast (AED 140) — 2 hours of Q&A with an Emirati host about culture, religion, daily life. Best 140 dirhams of the trip.
• Al Seef and Al Fahidi historic district — wandered the wind-tower lanes for 3 hours alone, had Karak chai for AED 5 at a tiny cafe, watched the sunset over the Creek from an abra. Zero touts.
• Solo dinner at Time Out Market — 18 stalls, communal seating, nobody bats an eye at a woman alone.
• Booked a small-group desert tour (Platinum Heritage, AED 595) — falconry display, private camp, traditional dinner. Half the group were women in their 40s and 50s.

What I skipped: beach clubs, Marina nightlife, Atlantis. Didn't miss any of it. Dubai has a quieter, slower layer if you ignore the Instagram version.

author: Meera
title: Visited Dubai during Ramadan as a non-Muslim solo traveller — surprisingly the best trip
content:
I had no other dates available so I went during Ramadan 2026 (mid-March). Everyone warned me. It was the calmest, kindest trip I've had anywhere.

Reality check on the rules:
• You can eat and drink in your hotel (any restaurant inside, any time of day) and in tourist-zone restaurants — most stay open with curtains/screens at the front. Just don't eat or drink while walking outdoors during fasting hours.
• Beach clubs and pools were open but quieter. No music until after iftar (sunset, around 6:45pm).
• Malls open later (10am instead of 9am) and stay open till 1am.
• Nightlife is fully closed for the month. No clubs, no live music, no DJs in bars.

The surprise: Iftar dinners are extraordinary. I booked 3 different ones (Bab Al Shams desert resort iftar AED 295, Arabian Tea House in Al Fahidi AED 110, my hotel's iftar buffet AED 175). Every single one was warmer and more communal than a normal restaurant meal. Strangers shared dates with me at the breaking of fast.

If you're not going for the party scene, Ramadan is genuinely the best time to visit. Quieter attractions, lower hotel rates, and you see the city's actual character.

author: Sneha
title: Did the desert safari solo — was paired with a couple's group, here's what actually happens
content:
Booked a standard evening safari with Arabian Adventures (AED 350) because it was the cheapest reputable one. Was nervous about being the only solo person.

What happens in practice: pickup van had 6 of us — me, a Korean honeymoon couple, a German solo guy in his 40s, and two French sisters. Nobody paired off awkwardly because the dune-bashing seats are 4 to a Land Cruiser anyway. Driver was professional. No staring, no "why are you alone" energy.

At the desert camp (about 80 people total) you're seated at low tables of 8. I sat with two solo women I'd just met in the van and we ended up eating dinner, watching the falconry show, and trying the henna together. The henna queue was 90% women travelling alone or with friends.

The only solo-specific awkwardness: the camel ride and the photo-with-falcon stations are clearly designed for couples to take pictures of each other. I asked a guide to take a photo of me and he did happily. Took about 10 seconds.

Don't book the "premium" or "VIP" safari (AED 700+) solo — those are explicitly couple/group-shared dining tables and you'll feel it. Standard tier is genuinely solo-friendly.

author: Aditi
title: Burj Khalifa 'At The Top' solo at 8am — completely worth it for the empty deck
content:
Bought the 'Early Bird' ticket online a week ahead — AED 169 for the 8:00am slot (vs AED 274 for sunset). Sunset slots are mobbed; mornings are 80% empty.

Got to the deck at 8:15am. Maybe 20 other people on the entire 124th-floor observation level. Could stand at the glass, take photos with no heads in frame, walk the full perimeter twice without queuing. The Dubai morning haze actually made the view dreamier than the harsh midday sun would have.

Entirely fine to be solo here. Most other early visitors were either solo or older couples. No one's trying to take a couple's selfie that you have to dodge. A staff photographer offered to take my photo at the railing — felt natural, not awkward.

The small thing nobody mentions: there's a pretty good cafe on level 122 (Atmosphere) that opens at 8:30am for breakfast. I ate alone at a table with a 360 view for AED 195. Felt like a really small luxury.

Would absolutely repeat the morning slot. Would never do sunset solo based on what I saw of the queues coming in as I left.

author: Kavya
title: Ate alone every dinner in Dubai for 6 nights — here's where it actually felt comfortable
content:
I'm a confident solo traveller but Dubai is heavy on couples and family vibes, so I was curious where it would land. Some places were genuinely great. A few I'd skip next time.

Felt completely normal solo:
• Time Out Market Souk Al Bahar — communal high tables, you blend in. AED 70–150 a meal.
• Operation: Falafel (multiple locations) — cheerful, fast, clearly used to solo diners. AED 35.
• Ravi Restaurant in Satwa — Pakistani institution, packed but staff didn't treat me weirdly. AED 25 for a full meal. Sat in the family section by request.
• Hotel breakfast buffets in general — every hotel I checked into had 2–3 women eating solo at any given time.

Felt awkward solo:
• Pier 7 (the cluster of restaurants on Marina) — every place is romantic-couple staging. Tables are 2-tops with views "for couples."
• Atlantis main restaurants — explicit honeymoon energy, hostess literally asked "just one?" with a face.
• Any rooftop bar with a "vibe" — by 9pm they're 90% couples and you feel like you're loitering.

The pattern: Dubai's casual + Emirati + South Asian places are completely fine solo. The aspirational "experience" restaurants are not. Save those for when you have company.

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Beach club solo entry — has anyone actually been turned away for coming alone?
content: I've read that some Dubai beach clubs turn solo travellers away or strongly prefer couples and groups. One blogger said she emailed Nikki Beach and they literally replied saying they "prefer couples and friend groups." Has anyone been refused entry as a solo woman in 2025/26? Which clubs are genuinely welcoming solo and which should I skip?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: What does "cover shoulders and knees in public" actually mean in Dubai Marina day-to-day?
content: I keep reading this rule but then I see Instagram photos of women in crop tops at JBR and Dubai Marina. Is this rule only enforced at souks and mosques or does it technically apply everywhere? I've packed summer clothes and don't want to offend anyone or get stopped — but I also can't find a clear answer on what "public" actually means here.

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Pink taxi vs Careem vs regular taxi — what do women actually use day-to-day and is the airport arrival safe at 11pm?
content: I know pink taxis (female drivers) exist but are they easy to book or do I need to pre-arrange? And is Careem genuinely as reliable as Uber? I'm arriving at DXB at 11pm and want to know exactly what to do on arrival — pre-paid booth, app, or is there a specific exit to head to?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Unmarried couple sharing a hotel room — do they actually ask for a marriage certificate or is that old information?
content: I keep seeing conflicting information. Some sources say Dubai fully decriminalised cohabitation for unmarried couples and hotels freely allow it. Others say some hotels can still demand a marriage certificate. What's actually happening on the ground in 2025/26? Has anyone been asked for proof of marriage at check-in recently?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Ladies' nights in Dubai solo — will they let me in alone or is it couples/groups only at the door?
content: I'm travelling solo and have been told Dubai ladies' nights are the best way to meet people. But will I actually be let into venues alone? Which places in JBR, Marina, or Downtown are genuinely welcoming to a solo woman versus ones that make you feel awkward walking in by yourself?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Is VPN use actually illegal in Dubai and what's the real risk for a tourist?
content: I need a VPN for work calls. I've read that VPNs are technically restricted in the UAE but also that millions of residents use them daily without issues. What's the actual risk for a tourist using one for 5 days? And does it matter which VPN or which websites I'm accessing?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Bikini at a public beach like JBR — genuinely fine or is that only for private beach clubs?
content: Every source says bikinis are fine at "private beaches and hotel pools." But what about the public JBR beach? I've seen women in bikinis there in photos. Is it actually fine or am I going to get in trouble? And what specifically counts as too revealing — is a standard two-piece fine?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Walking alone at night in Dubai Marina — is it actually as safe as everyone says?
content: I'm staying in Dubai Marina and want to walk back from dinner along the Marina Walk at 11pm. I've read Dubai is extremely safe at night but I want to hear from women who've actually done this. Is JBR and the Marina Walk at night genuinely fine solo or should I always take a cab after dark?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Can you genuinely do Dubai on a tight budget or is that completely unrealistic?
content: Every article says Dubai "can be done on a budget" but then lists activities that cost AED 200+ each. What does a real budget day look like — accommodation, food, transport, one activity — for a solo woman? I'm not backpacker-budget but I'm not luxury either. Give me real numbers.

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Female-only dorm hostels in Dubai — are they actually safe and social or is it mostly workers?
content: I found a few hostels on Hostelworld with female-only dorms. But one blogger said Dubai hostels are mostly workers looking for cheap housing rather than backpackers. Has anyone stayed in a female dorm in Dubai recently — is it safe, clean, and do you meet other travellers or feel isolated?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Visiting Dubai during Ramadan — miserable for a tourist or actually worth experiencing?
content: My only available dates fall during Ramadan 2026. I want to know what it's actually like: Can I eat and drink in public at all? Are restaurants closed during the day? Will beach clubs and nightlife be shut? Is the atmosphere hostile toward tourists or just different? Real experiences please, not just "respect the culture" advice.

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Travelling to Dubai with prescription antidepressants — do I need a letter from my doctor?
content: I've seen vague warnings about controlled medications in the UAE. I take a common SSRI daily. Do I need a doctor's letter to bring it in? Is there a quantity limit? And what exactly happens at customs — is it a routine check or only if flagged? Has anyone brought SSRIs or similar medication through DXB recently?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Desert safari as a solo woman — will I feel like a third wheel or is solo totally normal?
content: Every desert safari package seems marketed at couples or families. If I book solo will I be paired with random strangers in a group jeep? Is dune bashing and the camp dinner experience comfortable solo or does the whole thing feel awkward? Which operators are good for solo women specifically?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Unwanted attention in Dubai as a solo woman — is it really as rare as official sources claim?
content: Every travel guide says harassment is near-zero in Dubai. But I've read first-person accounts of women experiencing constant staring and unwanted approaches, especially outside tourist zones. One blogger even bought a fake engagement ring to reduce attention. What's the real day-to-day experience, particularly in older areas like Deira and Bur Dubai?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Is the Dubai Metro actually useful for tourist areas or do you end up in taxis for everything?
content: I want to use the metro as much as possible for cost and ease. Which major tourist spots are genuinely walkable from a metro station versus which ones require a taxi regardless? Specifically: getting from Downtown to JBR, to the Gold Souk in Deira, and to Palm Jumeirah.

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: eSIM for Dubai — Airalo vs Holafly vs buying a local SIM at the airport, what do women actually use?
content: I've seen recommendations for Airalo and Holafly but also for du and Etisalat SIMs bought at DXB. What's actually faster and more reliable for maps, WhatsApp video calls, and general data? And if I arrive late at night, is the airport SIM counter open or should I get an eSIM before I fly?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Visiting Jumeirah Mosque — do they provide an abaya or do I need to bring my own?
content: I want to visit on the open day. Do they provide an abaya and headscarf at the entrance or am I expected to bring my own? And how strict are they on what you wear underneath — can I wear trousers and a long-sleeved top, or do I need to be fully covered head to toe before even entering?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Social media posting from Dubai — is there genuinely a risk or is it massively over-exaggerated for tourists?
content: I create travel content and post daily. I've seen warnings that certain posts can get tourists arrested in the UAE — complaining about the country, anything political, certain images. What does this actually mean in practice for someone posting normal travel photos and honest opinions? Has anyone ever had a real issue?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Drinking age in Dubai is 21 — do they actually check ID at bars and beach clubs?
content: I know alcohol is 21+ in the UAE. Do venues actually card at the door? I'm 28 but look younger and I don't want to be turned away at a beach club or bar. Also — if I'm at a hotel pool day pass, can I order alcohol without any extra process?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Women-only hotel floors in Dubai — genuinely useful or just a marketing gimmick?
content: A few hotels advertise women-only floors with added security features. As a solo traveller I feel reasonably safe in Dubai already. Is there a practical difference — better locks, keycard-only lift access, female-only staff on that floor? Or is it mostly a marketing thing that doesn't change the actual experience?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Going to the Burj Khalifa solo — is it awkward or totally normal to do alone?
content: I want to do the 'At The Top' experience but every photo shows couples or families celebrating something. Is it genuinely weird to go solo? Also practically — do you need to book a specific time slot far in advance or can you get a same-day ticket, and is the early morning slot worth it for solo women (fewer crowds)?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Dubai in July — is the summer heat genuinely manageable or will I feel miserable the moment I step outside?
content: I can only travel in July. Temperatures are 40°C+. I've read that most tourism happens indoors in malls and air-conditioned spaces anyway, so it's doable. But is even getting from the taxi to the entrance unbearable? Is there anything outdoor that's worth doing in summer, or should I mentally write off all outdoor activities?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: What does food actually cost in Dubai — can I eat a proper sit-down meal for under AED 80?
content: I want real price ranges, not "Dubai can be expensive." I've seen shawarma recommended as a cheap eat. But what does a decent sit-down lunch at a non-touristy restaurant in Downtown or Deira actually cost? Is eating well for under AED 80 a meal realistic or am I looking at food courts and fast food only?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: How drunk is "too drunk" in Dubai if I'm drinking at a hotel bar and walking back to my room?
content: I know Dubai has zero-tolerance laws for public intoxication. But if I have a few drinks at my hotel's rooftop bar and then walk through the lobby back to my room, am I breaking a law? What about getting in a taxi after drinking? I want to understand what actually triggers a legal issue versus what's just having a normal evening.

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Can I buy alcohol from a supermarket in Dubai as a tourist or is that only for residents?
content: I've read that you now need a free licence to buy alcohol from shops in the UAE. But I've also read that tourist areas have licensed bottle shops where tourists can buy without one. What's the actual situation in 2025/26 — can I walk into a shop and buy wine, or do I need to only drink at hotel bars?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Tipping in Dubai — do I tip at restaurants where a service charge is already on the bill?
content: I've read that many Dubai restaurants add a mandatory 10% service charge. Do I still tip on top of that or does the charge go to staff? And what about taxis — do I round up or is a tip not expected? I don't want to be rude but I also don't want to double-tip if it's not necessary.

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Taking photos of people in the Gold Souk and old Deira — what are the actual rules?
content: I want to photograph the souks but I've read that photographing people without consent is illegal in the UAE. Does this apply to general market scenes or only if I'm clearly pointing at one person? What's the actual rule and how strictly is it applied to tourists with cameras or phones?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Codeine in Dubai — I've read it's a controlled substance. I have ibuprofen with codeine at home. Can I bring it?
content: I've seen a specific warning that medications containing codeine are controlled in the UAE and can result in arrest. I have standard over-the-counter co-codamol. Is this actually a serious issue or is the risk overstated for standard low-dose tablets? Should I just leave it at home entirely?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Visiting Dubai with my girlfriend — how careful do we need to be about displaying affection?
content: I'm a woman travelling with my female partner. Homosexuality is technically illegal in the UAE. We're not going to be openly affectionate but I want to understand the realistic risk level for tourists. Is holding hands in a tourist area like Dubai Marina something that draws attention or leads to problems? What do LGBTQ+ women travellers actually experience?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Eating alone as a solo woman in Dubai restaurants — is it comfortable or do you feel stared at?
content: I've travelled solo before and some cities make you feel incredibly conspicuous eating alone at a restaurant. What is the vibe in Dubai specifically? Are there restaurants where a solo woman eating alone is clearly normal and comfortable, or do you end up defaulting to food courts and delivery?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: What is the women-and-children metro cabin actually like — is it enforced and is it worth using?
content: I've read that Dubai Metro has dedicated women-and-children cabins at the front. Is this actively enforced, or is it just a suggestion? Is it noticeably more comfortable than the mixed cabins? And is it always the front carriage or does it vary by line?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Palm Jumeirah monorail and getting around without a car — is it actually doable?
content: I don't want to rely on taxis the whole trip but Palm Jumeirah looks like it needs a car. Is the monorail genuinely useful for getting around Palm, or is it only one route? And what's the best way to get to Atlantis from central Dubai without paying for a taxi the whole way?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Airbnb scams in Dubai — is this a real risk and what should I look out for?
content: One blogger specifically warned about Airbnb scams in Dubai being common. Is this genuine? Are there signs of a scam listing I should watch for? And is it safer to book through a known hotel or serviced apartment platform rather than Airbnb altogether for Dubai specifically?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Pregnancy outside of marriage in Dubai — I'm newly pregnant and travelling with my boyfriend. Is there any risk?
content: I've read a US State Department warning that pregnancy outside of marriage can result in arrest and detention in the UAE, and that doctors may ask for a marriage certificate during prenatal visits. We're not married. We're only visiting for a week, not seeking medical care. Should I be worried or is this only relevant if you're a resident?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Currency exchange at DXB airport vs in the city — where do I actually get the best rate?
content: I've heard the airport exchange rate is terrible and to wait until I'm in the city. But I'll arrive late at night. Is there a trustworthy exchange in central Dubai that's open late? Or is it better to get a small amount at the airport and exchange the rest at an exchange house in a mall the next morning?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: What is the Dubai Police App SOS feature and should solo women actually download it?
content: I've seen the Dubai Police App's SOS feature mentioned as a safety tool for solo women. How does it actually work — does it alert police with your location? Is it useful in practice or more of a PR feature? And what's the fastest way to actually get police help if something goes wrong?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Is jaywalking in Dubai actually enforced with fines or is that a myth?
content: I've read that jaywalking carries an AED 400 fine in Dubai and that cameras are everywhere. Is this genuinely enforced against tourists who don't know where the crossings are? Or is it like most cities where it's technically illegal but nobody actually gets stopped for it?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Global Village Dubai — is it worth going and what's the dress code as a tourist?
content: I've seen Global Village mentioned as a must-do. But I've also seen that it's a "family attraction" and the dress code is stricter than beach areas. What does that mean practically for what I wear? And is it genuinely worth the visit or more of a local thing that's underwhelming for international tourists?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Dhow cruise on the Creek solo — is this comfortable and is it a legitimate tourist experience or a tourist trap?
content: I want to do an evening dhow cruise on the Dubai Creek. Is this something that's comfortable to do solo? Will I be seated with other tourists or will I feel awkward at a table alone? And is it worth doing or is it heavily overpriced for what you get — dinner and a view?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: What's the best area to stay in Dubai as a solo woman — Downtown, Marina, or JBR, and does it matter?
content: I've narrowed it down to Downtown, Dubai Marina, or JBR. Which is best for a solo woman in terms of walkability at night, restaurants within walking distance, and easy metro/taxi access? Or does it not really matter that much and I should just go with price?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Time Out Market Dubai — is it genuinely good and is it solo-friendly?
content: I've seen Time Out Market in Downtown Dubai recommended for solo travellers specifically because you can eat alone without feeling conspicuous. Is this actually true? What's the price range, and how does it compare to just eating at a regular restaurant or food court in a mall?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Is the public bus in Dubai usable for tourists or is it really only metro and taxi?
content: I want to use public transport and understand Dubai has buses. But every travel guide seems to skip them entirely. Are public buses actually useful for tourist routes, or are they so irregular and confusing that it's not worth it? And do they require a Nol card or can you pay on board?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Smoking hookah in restaurants in Dubai — is this still actually legal indoors?
content: I've read that the rules around smoking shisha in restaurants changed. One source said smoking in restaurants is still happening. Another said it was restricted after certain regulations. What's the actual situation in 2025/26 — can restaurants still serve hookah indoors and is it common in the tourist areas?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Deira Gold Souk alone as a solo woman — is the haggling aggressive and will I feel pressured?
content: I want to visit the Gold Souk but I've heard the sales pressure can be intense. Is it manageable solo for a woman or does shopping alone mean you get approached more aggressively? And is it actually possible to buy something at a fair price without knowing how to negotiate gold pricing?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Poppy seeds, herbal supplements, CBD — what else can I accidentally bring into Dubai that's illegal?
content: I've seen a warning about poppy seeds being flagged as drug possession at Dubai customs. And CBD products are illegal in the UAE even if legal at home. What other everyday items do I need to double-check before packing? I want a practical list beyond the standard "no drugs" advice.

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Spa day in Dubai — what's the tipping etiquette and are there women-only spas?
content: I want to book a massage or spa treatment. Are there women-only spas or is it always mixed? And what's the tipping norm — I've read 10-15% is standard but some places already include a service charge. Do I ask before or after the treatment?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Solo woman at 40 in Dubai — will I feel out of place or is it genuinely for all ages?
content: Most Dubai travel content seems aimed at 20-somethings doing beach clubs and nightlife. I'm 40, not interested in clubs, more into cultural experiences, good food, and one desert safari. Is Dubai actually enjoyable for a solo woman in her 40s who's not chasing the party scene?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Is there a women-only beach in Dubai and how do I access it?
content: I've seen a women-only beach mentioned in Dubai — apparently at Jumeirah. How do I actually get there, is it free, and how does it work in terms of rules? And is it actually used or is it an empty beach most of the time?

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Dubai for 4 days solo — what's the realistic itinerary that isn't just malls and Burj Khalifa?
content: I'm planning 4 days and every article gives the same itinerary: Burj Khalifa, Dubai Mall, desert safari, Marina dinner. What else is actually worth doing that isn't on every list? I'm interested in older Dubai, food markets, and things that show the city beyond the luxury tourist bubble.

author: TripAdvisor Research
title: Abra water taxis across the Creek — how much should I pay and will I be overcharged as a tourist?
content: I want to take the abra across Dubai Creek between Deira and Bur Dubai. How much should the fare actually be and is there a fixed price? Will I be quoted an inflated tourist price, and how do I avoid that? Is there a fixed-price government ferry I should use instead?
